topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The package arrived just as the evening chill settled deep into my bones. It was wrapped in plain brown paper, tied with coarse twine, and inside, a bouquet of deep purple irises. My hands, usually steady, trembled slightly as I lifted the box. There was no note, no inscription, just the cool, damp scent of the flowers hitting my nose. I set it down on the kitchen counter, my gaze fixed on the unfamiliar arrangement. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to stretch and deepen, and a knot tightened in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I backed away slowly, my eyes darting towards the front door, half-expecting someone to materialize from the darkness outside.

[story 2]

When I found the arrangement on my desk, it was a startling burst of vibrant orange lilies. They were impossibly perfect, the petals thick and velvety. I picked one up, the stem feeling unnervingly cool against my fingertips. I turned the bouquet over and over, searching for any hidden message, any clue that might explain this unsolicited gesture. Silence hung heavy in the apartment; the usual city hum seemed muted, replaced by a high-pitched ringing in my ears. My heart began to pound a frantic rhythm against my ribs, a frantic drumming that drowned out every other sound. I dropped the flowers onto the rug, needing to put distance between myself and the silent, beautiful intrusion.

[story 3]

The delivery person left the flowers on my doorstep before dawn. They were white roses, pristine and unnervingly bright against the grey concrete. I hovered by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. Each passing car seemed too loud, every distant siren too sharp. I kept glancing down the street, my posture rigid, every muscle coiled tight. When I finally managed to open the box, the scent—sweet, cloying, almost suffocating—filled the small entryway. I didn't touch them immediately; instead, I stood frozen, my gaze locked on the stems, waiting for something to happen, waiting for the air to crackle.

My hands, usually steady, trembled slightly as I lifted the box. There was no note, no inscription, just the cool, damp scent of the flowers hitting my nose. I picked one up, the stem feeling unnervingly cool against my fingertips. I turned the bouquet over and over, searching for any hidden message, any clue that might explain this unsolicited gesture. My heart began to pound a frantic rhythm against my ribs, a frantic drumming that drowned out every other sound. I dropped the flowers onto the rug, needing to put distance between myself and the silent, beautiful intrusion.

The delivery person left the flowers on my doorstep before dawn. They were white roses, pristine and unnervingly bright against the grey concrete. I hovered by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. Each passing car seemed too loud, every distant siren too sharp. I didn't touch them immediately; instead, I stood frozen, my gaze locked on the stems, waiting for something to happen, waiting for the air to crackle.
